title: 2026/02/27 GA Subcommittee B [Part 3]
description: Friday 27 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee B Emergency Management Bill (No 2) ﻿Southland District Council - Cameron McIntosh, Chief Executive Gisborne District Council - Rehette Stoltz, Mayor - Nedine Thatcher-Swann, Chief Executive Orion New Zealand Limited - Vivienne Wilson, Policy Lead Stantec - Grant Maxwell, NZ Infrastructure Resilience Leader KRA Dog Scoops - Jeen Fourie, Owner NZ Airports Association - Billie Moore, Chief Executive - Steve Riden, Policy Director Animal Justice Party Aotearoa New Zealand - Danette Wereta, General Secretary Insurance Council of New Zealand - Sean Fullan, Disaster Response and Recovery Leader

chunks.text: Kira, thank you. Can you hear me loud and clear? Yeah. Fantastic. Hey, thank you for this opportunity. I'd just like to acknowledge you, uh, in the challenge that you have in front of you, listening to a lot of people shouting at you at at, at high speed. So, um, thank you for, for, uh, listening and look. I'm the Chief Executive of Southland District Council.

Uh, we represent a large rural hazard exposed, uh, district, approximately 20% of the south island, and that requires both strong local capability and effective national support. Uh, the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events has increased exacerbating Southland's exposure to hazards.

We went for nearly 20 years without a state of emergency, and then five were declared within six years. Uh, in 2020, we had two flooding events, 2023 and gore. There was an extensive flooding event. 2024, we had the blue cliffs, uh, hazard, uh, coastal hazard.

And 2025, uh, there was the widespread, uh, wind event. Uh, we do support the core intent of the bill, uh, being strengthened national leadership, better iwis partnership, and improved risk reduction and better recovery and welfare provisions.

However, while we are supportive of the intent, we are concerned about its practical implementation. Southern District Council needs a system that works for small, rural and remote communities, and the legislation must be practical, resourced and flexible.

So the key messages that I would like to share with you, uh, come under a couple of categories. First is the system leadership and governance. Uh, we would like to see that the relationships between central and local government are clarified. So those governance structures must clearly show how emergency management committees interact with councils, especially where funding, staffing, and policy decisions, uh, intersect.

With respect to the partnership with Iwi Maori, we, um, we strongly support that, uh, and, and consistent with TER and South End District Council's existing, uh, relationship and work with incorporated in ku. However, the system needs to avoid duplicating structures or creating confusion about representation.

So we request explicit flexibility, so partnership structures reflect the local context and the existing relationships. We, we also would like to see national versus regional versus local, uh, clarity and support, clearer role delineation and the use of the national lead agencies.

Um, operational overlap remains a concern, especially how national direction interacts with local autonomy, autonomy during fast moving events. I also want to just touch on the chief executive duties, which is a matter dear to my heart. Uh, the council chief executive responsibilities significantly expand under this bill, uh, requiring the guidance and creating pressure for small councils where chief executives may also serve as controllers or recovery managers.

And we also note that there is uncertainty in the transition timing with some things being delayed, uh, their commencement or key provisions being delayed, which, uh, could well lead to some uncertainty about when the responsibilities take effect, the matter of implementation, uh, the funding, the capability, and the capacity.

And this is one of the issues that, uh, is of the greatest concern to us. The new legislative requirements create substantial workload and resourcing challenges, and you will be keenly aware that we are also facing some other challenges in terms of other legislative demands and, uh, controls on the amount that councils are allowed to spend potentially.

So small and rural councils are already under great fiscal pressure and, uh, risk of unfunded mandates, uh, such as this one. Uh, add to that complication, the risk reduction, the reporting planning and engagement duties assume at administrative scale, many councils do not have. So we request that central government funding or an adjustment of obligations is made to match a realistic capacity.

We do strongly support elevating risk reduction, but risk assessments and resilient planning risks duplicates work already underway in long-term plans and spatial planning, climate adaptation, et cetera. And it overlaps with the planning natural environment and local government system improvements bills, and it risks inconsistent expectations.

So we, we, like many others, I suspect in local government, are requesting an alignment of the definitions of functions and the processes across all of the related legislation. The new concept of operational areas may create confusion in regions like Southland with dispersed communities and boundaries should generally follow existing structures unless a strong case exists otherwise.

And we support increased powers for controllers, but we, uh, urge accountability to elected members must remain and it must remain clear. And we would like to echo Minister Mitchell's recent comments. Uh, to that effect, shared controllers and recovery managers must continue to be enabled for small councils.

The community realities for a district like Southland, uh, and disproportionately affected communities, the bill uses a concept without explicitly defining it. And so we would request a clear definition that explicitly includes rural communities as well as seniors, disabled children, culturally and linguistically deser diverse communities, and those experiencing socioeconomic deprivation, the community and volunteer capacity.

Southland, rural Southland relies heavily on volunteers. The recent event, uh, that we experienced across Southland and Klu, uh, the, the loss of comms and, uh, the, the lack of any avail available electricity or support. The areas like Tiano, which were incredibly hard hit, mobilized themselves without communications and provided support to community members.

But we, we do rely on volunteers. So the bill needs to simplify and should simplify response planning requirements to better rec, recognize volunteering as a, as is fundamental to resilience. Um, and we also support inclusion of animal welfare, but we note the increased engagement and planning workload for rural districts with varied livestock types.

So in closing, Southland District Council supports the intent of the bill and is committed to strengthening emergency management. But to succeed, the new system must provide clear, workable governance, recognize local capacity constraints, avoid duplication across multiple reforms, and be fairly and sustainably resourced, uh, and, and, uh, deal with the inconsistencies between various bits of legislation.

For example, the chief executive's responsibilities under the local government act versus the chief executive's responsibilities under this bill. At the moment, we operate as many councils do. We are required to be economic development agencies, we're required to be infrastructure providers, and we are required to be regulators.

We're also now being plundered by central government to fund, uh, uh, the Commerce Commission and tomato ROI, uh, we would note that if this bill went in without having, um, suitable funding to go with it, instead of providing all those services and, and when required using our staff to support emergency management, we would in effect be setting up emergency management systems that sometimes do infrastructure and sometimes do community development and sometimes do regulation. Thank you.

Thank you, Cameron. We've got one, um, question for you from Andy Foster. Yeah, look, thanks, Cameron. Good to see you again. Um, just the, the issue that's been raised about the, you mentioned widening, CEO responsibility, and one of the conversations we've already had, uh, in the submission process is there isn't actually an opportunity there.

Uh, and that CEOs ultimately have a, a responsibility in terms of the district planning, uh, land use planning side of things, as well as the emergency management side. And, um, there's a real opportunity potentially, uh, to be able to align those two, which currently is being missed. And, uh, a lot of people have argued as being missed in this legislation too, needs to be addressed.

You've got a view on that that you can, you know, because you, you wear two hats, you can actually make sure those two things are aligned. And likewise, likewise, you're elected members. Look, my, my answer, Andy, and it's great question. My answer is we're, we are generally happy to try and do, uh, what we can. We just need it to be funded. Uh, so our, our challenge is gonna be how those inconsistencies are gonna be matched.

Um, if we set the organization up in line with, uh, delivering the national, you know, in, in line with this bill and all of the expectations in there, uh, we, we see some challenges around just how that would be afforded. Thanks. Thank you very much for your submission today, Cameron, and all the very best.

Thank you. Thank you. We now welcome Rashid, I think it's Rashid from Gisborne District Council, Rahi Rahi. Apologies. Rahi. There we go, RA, love. Lovely to see you. I'm Rahi Stoltz. I'm the mayor at the Gisborne District Council, and I have with me, um, our CEO Nadine Thatcher Swan, and we have got a presentation of about seven minutes that we would like to talk to you about, uh, GIIN District Council as a unitary authority, meaning we carry the full set of regional and territorial responsibilities, including all

of emergency management costs. Rafati covers over 8,000 square kilometers with 270 kilometers of coastline. Uh, we are sparsely populated, geographically isolated, highly exposed to seism seismic tsunami flooding and CLI climate driven, um, hazards.

And we have the highest proportion of Maori in any of any region or in alto or as a unit three authority. We cannot spread emergency management's costs across multiple councils. It is just us and increased statutory responsibilities had a direct and immediate impact on our rates.

This context underpinned many of our recommendations, and it is critical when assessing the both practicality. Um, I have lost count of how many times I have declared civil defense emergencies in our region. So it is very close to our hearts here. What we want to highlight to you, our first major concern is alignment across legislation.

Local authorities are required to manage hazard risk under multiple statuses, including the C act, the local government act, the Resource Management Act, and the forthcoming coming climate and planning reforms. All are currently being amended, all replaced. This BO Bill places a strong emphasis on risk reduction, but does not clearly differentiate risk reduction under emergency management from risk reduction functions delivered through planning infrastructure strategies and managed retreat.

So for a unitary authority, this creates duplication, inefficiency and extra cost, especially at a time where more unitary authorities are being encouraged. We are often required to undertake multiple parallel hazard assessments using different methodologies, standards, scopes, and governments governance arrangements to satisfy different legislative regimes.

Our submission supports stronger risk reduction, however, the bill must clearly define what risk reduction means in this context, ensure it aligns with planning and infrastructure responsibilities under other legislation and require consistent methodologies and standards.

So councils are not forced to duplicate the same work across multiple legal frameworks. Without this, the bill risks increasing compliance burden without improving outcomes, particularly for councils with li with limited capacity. Next, I wanna talk about central government support and implementation.

The bill introduces new responsibilities beyond response, including risk reduction, readiness and recovery, but does not provide funding mechanisms for these functions For unitary authorities, emergency management costs sit largely in operating expenditure and flow directly to rate payers with constrained rating capacity and proposed rate caps, there is a real risk that statutory obligations will not be deliverable.

So our, um, submission seeks central government co-funding for non-response emergency management functions, particularly hazard data modeling and risk reduction, and also the implementation support through excessive accessible standards, templates and guidance.

We also strongly support that any standards or rules adopted under the bill must be freely accessible, not not behind paid subscriptions, which would shift national system costs onto councils and communities. Our fourth point is MANA participation, structured and accountable.

Ang is an active and living partnership in MAN plays essential governance, operational, and community leadership roles in emergency management. The bill strengthens Maori participation in some areas, but limits mandated involvement at governance levels to statutory roles with core coordinating executive groups, which are not decision-making bodies.

Our submission six one discretion for emergency management committees to invite MANA to appointed to appoint representatives. And two, the ability for MANA to appoint their own representatives to the kick or the coordinating executive groups. For a unitary authority, it is critical that participation is meaningful, it's structured, continues and accountable rather than ad hoc.

The bull should enable locally appropriate arrangement without imposing blanket or unworkable governance models. My second to last point is localized declarations for unitary authorities. The bill intends to enable localized states of emergencies, but as drafted, this does not work well for unitary authorities that do not operate with ward boundaries.

The legislation assumes district or ward based geographic units, but in practice, this constraints our ability to make declarations that are proportionate and targeted to the actual communities affected. Our submission recommends explicitly enabling declarations for defined localities, settlements, or geographic areas.

This is a practical change that would allow the bill to function as intended across all local authority structures, including our unitary authorities. And then last but not least, permanent recovery powers and national leadership. Our, our final point, um, addresses recovery.

Our experience in demonstrates that recovery is no longer an exceptional or short term phase repeated and compounding events have created prolonged recovery environments that sit uncomfortably between response and business as usual. Regulation. The current approach relies on ad hoc event specific legislation.

While effective this introduces delays, uncertainty, and inconsistencies, we recommend embedding a standing recovery framework within the emergency management bill, and also establishing a permanent national recovery entity, or clearly designating a single lead agency with responsibility to recovery, funding, coordination, policy leadership, and system stewardship.

For unit three authorities managing prolonged recovery with constrained capacity. Permanent and predictable recovery arrangements are critical to delivering timely and equitable outcomes for our communities. In closing, the Gman District Council supports the intent of the bill, but seeks targeted amendments to ensure it is workable for unit three authorities and high risk unit, um, regions.

Our recommendations focus on clarity on integration, affordability, and practical implementation. We believe these changes will strengthen the emergency management system and approve outcomes for communities across Alt New Zealand. We are happy to answer any questions if you have any. Thank you.

Thank you, Sue, you both? Yes. Tim has a question. Andy did have one over. Tim, Thank you. Um, I just ask about the council, I apologize I don't have the coverage for my camera to be on, but, um, a number of councils have talked about sort of conflicting things between a, a, a local or district response and a regional response.

Do, do you see it as an advantage to be a unitary council? Does that give you clarity that you, just speaking clarity, does that give the clarity of, uh, I guess we'd call it unit of command or just one, one agency, one controller for the entire region? Um, I, I'll respond to that. Thank you for your question.

Yes, absolutely. We do see it as an advantage for, uh, our region in that you've got, yeah, really one clear, um, line of sight for that accountability, uh, also for the community in terms of their continuity and understanding, you know, who's, who's running the show pretty much.

Thank you. Thank you. Thanks, Tim. Andy, did you have a question? Oh, I'll, I'll ask, I'll ask one. I I had my hand up. I hadn't taken it down. Um, thanks, Rohit. Um, just the one question you, um, had about localized declarations, is this something that's in the bill itself, which is different to what's there at the moment?

Because, uh, uh, the only declaration I ever got to make not quite as busy as you were in terms of emergencies, um, is, uh, it wa was a very small part of our South coast, so it's not, wasn't a ward at all. Um, it was just particular, uh, a number of streets that faced the South coast, so we weren't prevented from doing that. Are you saying there's something in the legislation which prevents you, um, doing it by anything that o other than by ward or by the district as a whole?

Yeah, so that, so if I respond to that, so with our area just recently that was impacted was the top of the East Cape, which is bay. Um, so the legislation required, um, so we were talking about can we just declare, uh, for that particular area as a, as a unitary region, uh, but the legislation only allows it to be a district or, um, or a ward within that area.

So it's just really around the nuancing and that enable us to declare just for that specific area. That's clear. Thanks. Thank you to you both for your submission. Have a good day. No Worries. Thank you. Bye. Thank you. Welcome, Sue. We now have Orion New Zealand Limited, and we welcome Vivian for your 10 minute submission.

Welcome, Vivian. Uh, good afternoon. Thank you very much for this opportunity to, to appear before the committee and speak to our submission on the bill. My name's Vivian Wilson, and I am the policy lead at Orion New Zealand Limited. So just a little bit of information about Orion.

Orion is the third largest electricity distribution business in New Zealand. Our network extends from banks peninsula to Arthur's Pass from the M River to the river. We have over 229,000 customer connections, over 14,300 kilometers of lines and cables, and over 90,000 power poles.

And we currently have an important role under the Civil Defense Emergency Management Act as a lifeline utility. So we acknowledge that our community is increasingly dependent on our electricity distribution service. So it's essential we identify and manage our key risks.

Our community especially depends on electricity during and after high impact, low prop probability events such as major earthquakes or floods. Orion is currently preparing an application to the Commerce Commission for a customized price path, and this is to make sure that we have the revenue needed to provide a safe, reliable, resilient, and future ready network for our customers.

And we will make our application in June this year. And a key component of our customized price path application will be investing more to strengthen the Orion network so it can better withstand severe weather and natural disasters. And our customers have told us that keeping power safe, reliable, and resilient is what matters most, while also keeping costs as low as possible.

So Orion supports the new emergency management bill, and we very much appreciate the changes that have been made when comparing, comparing this bill with the 2023 bill. So I just wanted to highlight a few points of our submission. Uh, we are concerned about the widening of the duty of an essential infrastructure provider in clause 74 a, whereas the current position is that every lifeline utility must ensure that it is able to function to the fullest possible extent, even though this may be at a reduced level during and

after an emergency. The new duty is that we will need to ensure that our infrastructure is able to function to the fullest possible extent, et cetera. So it seems to us that this will be of a higher standard or a wider standard, and it may be significantly harder to meet that, given the definition of infrastructure components, which make up the definition of essential infrastructure.

So infrastructure components means assets, information networks, systems, suppliers, people and processes. So it is quite broad and we ask that the select committee, um, consider reverting to the previous formulation in section 60 of the C-D-E-M-A.

Um, in terms of sector response plans, Orion supports the development of sector response plans, but we reinforce the submission made by electricity networks RTRR, that the bill should be amend to provide that the director general must not require the development of a sector response plan during a state of emergency or a transition period.

And we've also raised some concerns about how a sector response plan may address default information sharing for the essential infrastructure providers. And that's in clause 1 0 5 B of the bill. We agree that information sharing is very important, but it can present some legal difficulties where some information is subject, subject to contractual provisions, privacy, and other considerations.

And we also need to be assured that Orion's information will be treated with the necessary protections. Another provision we would like to see clarified is clause 130 of the bill. Clause one 30 sets out various powers to close roads and public places during an emergency.

We support this clause, but we would like to see clear provision for allowing certain persons or groups of persons into restricted areas. We need to be able to get our authorized staff and contractors to our network quickly to affect repairs while also maintaining safety. And we think the bill would benefit from this clarification.

And we have some suggested wording here. We have made some comments on the compliance provisions in the bill as an EDB, we are subject to regimes under the Commerce Act and the Electricity Industry Act, and we are concerned about the complexities of another compliance regime and additional costs.

However, we do think that if a compliance regime is added, the functions of the director general of emergency management should also be brought in to include a capability, capability building function for persons who have responsibilities under the act, and that would match the similar regime under the Water Services Act.

Finally, we've made some comments in relation to the new rulemaking power for reporting requirements. We support the consultation process before the minister makes rules, and we think the provisions relating to reporting could be clarified to make it expressed that rules do not impose new reporting requirements on parties other than those already specifically referenced in the act.

And they would be the Emergency Management Committees, the National Controller regional controllers, the National Recovery Manager, and Regional Recovery Managers. So just to summarize, we very much support the bill, but three key takeaways from us would be to reconsider the change to the duty of essential infrastructure providers to provide more clarity around sector response plans and include clear provision for allowing certain persons or groups of persons into restricted areas.

Thank you. Thank you, Vivian. Uh, any questions? I have Tim, Timmy, can you hear us? Oh, I can. And sorry, I did mean to take my hand down, but I'm very interested in the, um, the definition and, uh, of the essential infrastructure, and we were saying infrastructure that needs to have that continuity.

Um, can you give us a tangible example of what would be brought in by this act that you would be responsible for that wasn't previously under the old definition? I think it would be suppliers. So the definition is quite broad now, because we would actually have to make sure somehow that suppliers could also function to provide that infrastructure.

So we're just, we're trying to work through that to see how that would apply, but we, we do have concerns that it, that is quite broad. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks, Jim. Andy? Yeah, thanks. Uh, ViiV. Nice to see you. Nice to you too. Yeah, look, I just, one question on that, the whole issue of how resilient the infrastructure and the systems are and how transparent, um, that is, uh, to, uh, to consumers and users.

Um, you, you've mentioned the role of ComCom there and, um, obviously asking for, uh, presumably, presumably the ability to be able to invest more in resilience, but how transparent is that process, um, in terms of the, your asset management and your, and your practices, um, so that customers can look at it and go, yeah, we can, we can see what you're doing, we can see why costs are going up because you're investing in, um, um, reliability and, uh, and resilience, et cetera.

Yep. So the asset management plan that we are required to do under the Commerce Commission requirements, that has quite, um, detailed requirements about what asset management we do need to disclose and be transparent about, and our resilient and our preparations for resilient infrastructure.

So that is quite substantial. Um, also at Orion, we're currently introducing a new integrated asset management system, and that's going to really improve things for us. So we'll be able to see where all our assets are, the state of the assets, and it, it integrates with the whole of our systems here at Orion.

And that's coming into force gradually over the next year. And so To, to just to follow that up, does that mean at the moment you're not fully across, uh, the state of your assets? We do, we are fully across the state of our assets, but we we're just using different systems and what we are trying to do is bring it in under one system, which will make it a lot easier, I think.
Okay. No thanks. Thank you, Vivian. Thank you for your submission today. All the very best. Thank you. We now welcome Grant Maxwell from Stantec. Welcome, grant

over to you for your 10 minutes submission. Got off. Um, thank you for having me. Uh, today, grant Maxwell, um, I'm from Stantec. Um, we're a large multi, uh, discipline engineering and consulting business, um, with many offices across the country. Um, we've been involved with lots of different pieces of, uh, I guess emergency management over the many years and personally, um, sort of 20 odd years playing in response, uh, and preparedness and recovery programs.

So, uh, really keen and thank you very much for your time. Um, overall, we're really in support of the intent of the emergency management bill revision, um, and its response to lessons from recent multi-regional events. Um, we have seen a consistent feature in, in the recent emergencies being very ad hoc engagement of what is the private sector, particularly professional and technical advisors.

Uh, in the absence of clear legislative frameworks. This creates a two system risks, uh, hesitation with critical advice being delayed because people are unsure how whether to engage and also disaster privateering, uh, rogue and unstructured land grabbing of various inputs through the process, um, that our sector is largely not proud of.

Uh, this is usually the wrong person at the wrong place at the wrong time. Neither of these outcomes serve our communities well, and they're both systems of a system that relies on goodwill and improvisation rather than preparedness by design. Uh, today I want to talk through three practical parts of our, um, submission, um, that is reducing ad hoc engagement for our industry, improving preparedness, uh, and enabling the system to confidently draw on capability that it already has, especially within the first 72 hours of a disaster.

Uh, point number one is the explicit recognition of professional and technical advisors as essential partners. The bill doesn't explicitly recognize professional and technical advisors. Despite the critical role we play across all phases of emergency management. In practice, these advisors are engaged immediately in emergencies, often before formal declarations or written delegations are in place, um, and are central to life safety decisions.

Uh, asset access and infrastructure continuity. The lack of recognition leads to ad hoc engagement and rather inconsistent integration into the Sims system with uneven outcomes between regions, uh, differing access to specialist capability, uh, particularly in environments where they're cut off.

Um, work is already underway, um, in this space. Um, Stantec has been involved with Nelson Tasman emergency management, uh, leading in NEMA resilience funded, uh, project to integrate professional services into the emergency response. This approach aligns with this existing sims escalation levels and SIM systems.

Uh, it preserves business as usual arrangements, uh, and introduces the structure coordination when the resource limits are exceeded. It has broad agreement across c DM groups, uh, that this model should be implemented more widely to address a more coordinated, uh, series of issues that continue to exist in our system. We've developed a national working group through ACE nz, uh, to collect and collaborate the industry.

This group includes mb uh, engineering New Zealand, and a selection of large consulting firms. Um, and I guess my message, uh, across this is, uh, as much as what's in the, uh, submission is that the capability in the system already exists. Uh, the sector is desperately looking for clarity, um, and explicit, explicit recognition in the bill with lock in good practice already being developed and improved preparedness nationwide.

Uh, number two is providing protection for those technical advisors. Acting in good faith recognition alone is insufficient if the advisors are not protected when engaged early, um, the bill expands liability protections well, but these are largely contingent on formal delegation, written declaration directions and declared emergencies in relevance.

Technical advice is often sought before these thresholds are met. Uh, and this advice is given in the field, uh, with incomplete information available and under time pressure with very adverse conditions for businesses and professional service providers. This creates legal hesitation, delayed engagement, slower decision making at critical moments.

This is not about reducing our accountability. It's about providing, uh, and protecting good faith technical judgment, uh, given to protect life safety and essential services. The message I have is that the system expects early expert input. Um, it must identify and manage that, uh, the legal hesitation that suppresses that, and that recognition without protection undermines our ability to be well prepared.

Uh, third point, uh, is around the expectations of the bill creating, uh, that will create a funding and capability gap. The bill appropriately raises expectations around planning and preparedness, coordination, consistency, digital enablement, and infrastructure resilience. However, the new statutory expectations without funding does create us risk for the future.

Those key issues are around capability and resourcing. Um, uneven across regions, smaller or rural regions struggling to meet higher standards, uh, and the preparedness activities are often discretionary without dedicated funding, um, made available for them. Funding uncertainty does lead to slower decision making, uh, in our experience, hesitation to engage professional services early.

Uh, increased reliance on reactive procurement, uh, which reinforces this disaster privateering. And it oftentimes, uh, a bill at the end that nobody wants to pay for. Preparedness costs money up front, but it is far cheaper than recovery. Uh, does, does it delivers faster, more confident response and transition decisions.

And the message is if government wants to take a step change and outcomes, expectations must be matched with funding and implementation support in conclusion, um, they all lead to the same outcome. Better preparedness, uh, recognizing technical advisors, protecting early advice and funding expectations will reduce a hoc engagement, avoids disaster privat hearing in our sector, uh, and enables better decisions that are made earlier.

And with confidence preparedness is where the bill can deliver its greatest return, not just in response, but for communities in the weeks and months and years. That follow. That was quite rush, wasn't it? Thank you. Uh, hopefully, uh, any questions? Welcome to them. Oh, thanks Grant. Really, um, helpful to listen to your submission today.

Andy, question, then I can hand back to Tim. Yeah, look, um, thank you very much, grant, really appreciate that. Um, just talking about that, um, issue of, uh, ad hoc, um, involvement, I, is this something that you, you are wanting a collection of professional, uh, um, advisors being, um, recognized explicitly in the bill in terms of maybe a, a schedule?

So you don't wanna get it wrong and put the wrong people in there, but you wanna get it right and put the right people in there. Yeah. Uh, good question. Uh, Andy, thank you for that. Um, look, the, the system knows, I think, I don't think we need to be as explicit as people in names. I think, you know, we're talking about organizations, so iwi well, um, represented and being brought into decision making, um, likewise professional services, um, in that shape and form that I guess it's dependent also in the region and what they need.

at the right, at the same time. Yeah, Look, if, if there are some words, um, that you can suggest to us, that will be very much a help, uh, a help. Cool. Thank you. Thank you very much for your submission today, grant. Thank you everyone. Kiaa Kia. Thank you, Tim.

Um, things that I discovered is that at the moment, animals are considered property that opens up a huge window for, for animals to be put into a consumerism space where people neglect animals, people treat animals as, uh, expendable.

So during Hurricane Katrina, which devastated Louisiana and Mississippi in 2005, companion animals were not permitted on evacuation transport or in emergency shelters.

As a result, thousands of people refused to evacuate, placing themselves and first responders at very high risk. At the same time, those who did evacuate were forced to leave their companion animals behind. And an estimated 250,000 animals were abandoned and fewer than 5% were ever re were, um, were ever reunited with their families.

So this was not an unavoidable tragedy, it was a planning failure, and it led to the passage of the Pets Evacuation and Transportation Standards Act. The reform that followed, uh, followed a catastrophic failure and we can legislate proactively. Instead. New Zealand now has the opportunity to avoid repeating that mistake, and we don't need to look overseas to see the gaps.

Climate related disasters are increasing in both frequency and severity. New Zealand has experienced repeated extreme weather events in recent years, floods, cyclones, wildfires, consistent with the documented impacts of human driven climate change. If sentient applies in ordinary law, it must also apply in emergencies. There is no formal plan after animals are rescued. So we need a 30 day holding period microchip, scanning, vet sign off, euthanasia where possible. And a reunification system as disaster really does separate families.

Red tape can delay rescues. So requiring guardian contact before entry puts animals at risk. For example, when water is rising, seconds really do matter. Assistant animals are ignored. In my role, I work closely with insurance industry stakeholders, including the emergency management sector, to support insurers responding to significant disaster events on behalf of the insurance council and our members, thanks for the opportunity today to make an oral submission to the committee.

Um, my comments will focus on two main components of our submission on the bill. First. Insurers and insurance products play a critical role in the response to and the recovery from disasters. And for this reason, the insurance industry is a key partner in the readiness for future recoveries. And as insurance fundamentally is about the acceptance, pricing, and pooling of risk.

Insurers advocate strongly for risk reduction, ICNZ and our members are therefore vital participants in A four R approach to emergency management. The insurance sector supports the government's efforts to strengthen New Zealand's emergency management system. We especially welcome the principle of a whole of society approach to emergency management introduced in the government's response to the inquiry, into the response to the North Island weather events.

While the bill makes improvements and moves the system toward a whole of society involvement, we believe there are opportunities to include additional partners in the private sector, including insurers. Um, some of this work is already underway as the Insurance council have partnered with NEMA and the Natural Hazards Commission at the national level to work together on pre-disaster recovery planning.

However, given the decentralized structure of emergency management roles and responsibilities, more work is needed at the regional and local level. The bill presents an opportunity to create pathways to better integrate insurers into planning and develop arrangements to foster increased certainty and therefore efficiency in both response and recovery.

Our submission details suggested amendments to specific clauses to achieve this, and I won't go into detail on all of those here. However, I would like to highlight two potential outcomes of collaboration and engagement with insurers of particular importance to the insurance sector are pre-agreed arrangements to enable information sharing between emergency management and the insurance sector, and developing a means such as accreditation to support insurers, access to affected areas, including properties following certain types of events.

Moving onto a second component of the industry's feedback on the bill. We urge the government to integrate emergency management reform with other legislative reforms that are proceeding throughout the system. Um, we all know that New Zealand is a risky country exposed to a wide range of natural hazards, and we expect climate change will continue to increase risks in the decades to come.

As I alluded to earlier, insurance is about pricing and accepting the transfer of risk and increasing risk, therefore may mean increasing pressure on the accessibility of insurance products. Insurers are committed to keeping iur insurance products accessible to all New Zealanders, but this must be done sustainably. Prudent and proactive management of natural hazard risk can positively influence insurance accessibility, supporting the economy and ultimately New Zealand's prosperity.

Now and in the long term future. We therefore encourage the government to consider the bill in the wider context of other reforms, especially RMA reform in the planning and national environment bills and the national adaptation framework, and to coordinate all legislation systematically, um, in the direction of managing and reducing natural hazard risk.

Integration of the emergency management reforms through the bill with these other legislative packages, uh, can ensure clarity before and after events and signal to individuals, businesses, and communities how New Zealand intends to manage natural hazard risk in the future. From an insurance perspective, we know from engaging with our members and reinsurers overseas that these signals matter to the global insurance capital market, demonstrating that we're growing and developing sustainably and sensibly managing natural hazard risks supports new

Zealand's access to reinsurance, a key driver of insurance accessibility. And finally taking a broader view of all policy decisions, building or at least supporting resilience and natural hazards should be considered across all government policies. Even those with objectives not directly aimed at managing risks. Insurers strongly support growth and development and we suggest that, for example, policy is aimed at enabling growth and future development should adopt a do no harm approach to resilience.

Avoiding one step forward and two steps back. Ultimately through growth that takes natural hazard risk into account and proactively manages that risk.
